examples.title: Find File Size Using fseek and ftell
examples.description: Program to calculate and display the size of a file in bytes
examples.explanation: Opens file in binary mode, seeks to end using SEEK_END with zero offset, then ftell() returns the position which equals total file size in bytes.

examples.title: Read Nth Record from Random Access File
examples.description: Program to read a specific record (by number) from a file of fixed-size records
examples.explanation: Opens file in binary read mode, calculates byte offset for the nth record using (n-1)*sizeof(record), uses fseek() to position, then fread() retrieves the record.

examples.title: Update Record in Random Access File
examples.description: Program to modify an existing record at a specific position
examples.explanation: Opens file in r+b mode for read/write, searches sequentially but can also seek directly using (id-1)*sizeof(emp), uses fseek() to move back one record, updates with fwrite(), and fflush() ensures immediate disk write.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) worst case, O(1) if using direct access by ID

examples.title: Display Records in Reverse Order
examples.description: Program to read and display all records of a file in reverse order without storing in memory
examples.explanation: Finds file size using fseek+ftell, calculates total number of records, then iterates from last to first using fseek() to position each time and fread() to retrieve records. No arrays used - processes one record at a time.

examples.title: Random Access File in Python
examples.description: Python equivalent demonstrating random access using seek() and tell()
examples.code:
import os

# Define record structure (similar to C struct)
RECORD_SIZE = 40  # bytes per record

def get_file_size(filename):
    '''Find file size using seek and tell'''
    with open(filename, 'rb') as f:
        f.seek(0, 2)  # Seek to end (SEEK_END = 2)
        return f.tell()

def read_record(filename, record_num):
    '''Read nth record (1-based index)'''
    with open(filename, 'rb') as f:
        # Calculate offset: (record_num - 1) * size
        offset = (record_num - 1) * RECORD_SIZE
        f.seek(offset)  # Random access to position
        data = f.read(RECORD_SIZE)
        return data

def update_record(filename, record_num, new_data):
    '''Update specific record'''
    with open(filename, 'r+b') as f:
        offset = (record_num - 1) * RECORD_SIZE
        f.seek(offset)
        f.write(new_data)
        f.flush()

def display_reverse(filename):
    '''Display records in reverse order'''
    file_size = get_file_size(filename)
    num_records = file_size // RECORD_SIZE
    
    with open(filename, 'rb') as f:
        for i in range(num_records, 0, -1):
            f.seek((i - 1) * RECORD_SIZE)
            record = f.read(RECORD_SIZE)
            print(record)
examples.explanation: Python's file seek() and tell() work identically to C's fseek() and ftell(). seek(offset, origin) where origin: 0=SEEK_SET, 1=SEEK_CUR, 2=SEEK_END. Python handles binary mode similarly.
